title: Griggs v. Duke Power Company
summary: The Supreme Court held that Title VII prohibits employment practices that are facially neutral but have a discriminatory impact unless the employer can show business necessity.
outcome_summary: The decision became a foundational precedent against employment screening rules that disproportionately excluded Black workers without sufficient justification.
impact_notes: Key disparate-impact precedent in employment discrimination law.

scores.notes: Landmark employment discrimination case under Title VII.

sources.source_title: Griggs v. Duke Power Company, 401 U.S. 424 (1971)
sources.notes: Primary decision summary and case record

title: Milliken v. Bradley
summary: The Supreme Court limited interdistrict desegregation remedies by ruling that suburban districts could not be included in a metropolitan desegregation plan absent proof that they had contributed to the constitutional violation.
outcome_summary: The decision sharply constrained metropolitan school desegregation efforts and is widely associated with limiting remedies for racially isolated urban schools.
impact_notes: Commonly viewed as a major limitation on school desegregation remedies.

scores.notes: A major setback for broad school desegregation efforts.

title: Regents of the University of California v. Bakke
summary: The Supreme Court struck down a rigid admissions quota while also allowing race to be considered as one factor among many in higher education admissions.
outcome_summary: The decision both limited explicit quota systems and preserved a narrower path for race-conscious admissions, making its effect on Black educational access mixed.
impact_notes: Historically influential but internally mixed in effect and later narrowed by subsequent case law.

sources.source_title: Regents of the University of California v. Bakke, 438 U.S. 265 (1978)
Regents of the University of California v. Bakke, 438 U.S. 265 (1978)

title: Grutter v. Bollinger
summary: The Supreme Court upheld the University of Michigan Law School's limited use of race as one factor in a holistic admissions process.
outcome_summary: The decision preserved a path for race-conscious admissions policies that many institutions viewed as important to Black representation in higher education, though later case law severely limited it.
impact_notes: Historically important pro-affirmative-action decision, though later narrowed.

scores.notes: Important but no longer fully intact precedent.

title: Gratz v. Bollinger
summary: The Supreme Court struck down the University of Michigan's undergraduate admissions system because its automatic point allocation for underrepresented minority applicants was not narrowly tailored.
outcome_summary: The ruling narrowed the forms of race-conscious admissions universities could use, limiting one direct tool institutions had used to support Black representation in higher education.
impact_notes: Important limiting decision issued the same day as Grutter; it constrained mechanical affirmative-action systems while leaving individualized review intact.

scores.notes: Meaningful restriction on affirmative-action admissions design, though narrower than a full ban.

sources.source_title: Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard/UNC Resources
sources.notes: Academic explainer placing Gratz within the longer affirmative action doctrine line later revisited in Fisher and Students for Fair Admissions.

title: Parents Involved in Community Schools v. Seattle School District No. 1
summary: The Supreme Court struck down certain voluntary K-12 student assignment plans that used individual racial classifications to promote school integration.
outcome_summary: The ruling limited one set of tools school districts could use to pursue racial integration in public education.
impact_notes: Generally viewed as constraining voluntary integration efforts in K-12 schools.

scores.notes: Important limit on voluntary race-conscious K-12 integration policies.

sources.source_title: Parents Involved in Community Schools v. Seattle School District No. 1, 551 U.S. 701 (2007)

title: Fisher v. University of Texas at Austin (2013)
summary: The Supreme Court vacated the lower-court ruling that had upheld the University of Texas admissions plan and required courts to apply genuine strict scrutiny to race-conscious admissions policies.
outcome_summary: The ruling tightened judicial review of affirmative-action admissions programs, creating additional legal risk for policies used to support Black representation in higher education without outlawing such programs outright.
impact_notes: Important doctrinal tightening decision: it did not ban race-conscious admissions, but it raised the scrutiny standard that universities had to survive.

scores.notes: Mixed-impact ruling that tightened doctrine without eliminating affirmative action.

sources.source_title: Fisher v. University of Texas at Austin, 570 U.S. 297 (2013)
Fisher v. University of Texas at Austin, 570 U.S. 297 (2013)
Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard/UNC Resources
sources.notes: Academic explainer connecting Fisher I to the longer Bakke-Grutter-SFFA line in affirmative action doctrine.

title: Fisher v. University of Texas at Austin (2016)
summary: The Supreme Court upheld the University of Texas admissions plan after applying strict scrutiny, concluding that the university had shown a sufficient basis for its limited use of race in admissions.
outcome_summary: The ruling preserved a narrowing but still meaningful legal path for race-conscious admissions policies that institutions viewed as relevant to Black educational access before the later Harvard and UNC decision.
impact_notes: Important late-stage reaffirmation of affirmative-action doctrine, though the holding was later overtaken by Students for Fair Admissions.

scores.notes: Positive but time-limited preservation of race-conscious admissions authority.

sources.source_title: Fisher v. University of Texas at Austin, 579 U.S. 365 (2016)
Fisher v. University of Texas at Austin, 579 U.S. 365 (2016)
Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard/UNC Resources
sources.notes: Academic explainer connecting Fisher II to the doctrine line later displaced by Students for Fair Admissions.
